items.q: What are the three elements of Beck's negative triad?
items.correct: Negative views of self, world, and future.
items.misconception: Negative views of past, present, and future.
items.why: Students often substitute 'past' for 'self' or 'world'. The triad is about the DOMAINS of negative thought (self/world/future), not time periods.

items.correct: Removing an unpleasant stimulus to INCREASE a behaviour.
items.misconception: Punishing a behaviour to decrease it.
items.random_wrong: Giving a reward to decrease behaviour.
items.why: Students confuse negative reinforcement with punishment. Both involve something unpleasant, but reinforcement (positive or negative) always INCREASES behaviour; punishment decreases it.

items.q: Why is high IQ a problem for the statistical infrequency definition?
items.correct: It is statistically rare but not abnormal/undesirable.
items.misconception: Because high IQ is actually common.
items.why: Students miss that statistical infrequency lacks a value judgement - it just measures rarity. High IQ is rare but desirable, exposing the definition's limitation.

items.q: Which symptom type involves loss of normal function?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Negative. “Positive” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: In Cheniaux 2009 how many of 100 patients got matching DSM/ICD diagnoses?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: 26. “13” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Approximately what percent of schizophrenia patients are co-morbid with depression (Buckley 2009)?
items.why: The real figure is 50. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
